name: Konnor Griffin
tldr: Griffin is a freaky five-tool superstar with big power and enough contact ability to weaponize it. He's also incredibly fast and has quickly developed into a plus shortstop. He's about to be one of the best young players in the game.

name: Jesús Made
tldr: Made is a do-it-all, wunderkind infielder with a shot to develop plus contact, power, and defense. He's the odds on favorite to be the sport's top prospect a year from now.

name: Nolan McLean
tldr: McLean is the best pitching prospect in baseball, and he dominated in his initial big league audition.

name: Samuel Basallo
tldr: Basallo has a plus throwing arm and top of the scale power that he's gotten to consistently in the minors, but his plate approach and ball blocking still need work.

name: Kevin McGonigle
tldr: McGonigle arguably has the best hit tool in the minor leagues, and he projects to have above-average game power. He's one of the top prospects in baseball, even if he has to slide off shortstop.

name: Leo De Vries
tldr: De Vries projects to stay at short and could hit 30 homers at maturity. His ceiling is very high.

name: Max Clark
tldr: Clark is a five-tool talent and is the top center field prospect in baseball.

name: Trey Yesavage
tldr: Yesavage has an elite splitter and carved up some of the best hitters on the planet during an incredible 2025 postseason run.

name: Thomas White
tldr: White is one of a few minor league pitchers whose pitches played like plus or better offerings across the board in 2025. He lacks precise feel for command, but might be too overwhelming for that to matter very much. He has no. 2 starter upside.

name: Bubba Chandler
tldr: Chandler's command and slider consistency are pretty raw, but his athleticism and background (former multi-sport & two-way player) allow for projection in these areas. He has prototypical size and arm strength, and his changeup has improved a ton as a pro. He's a potential front end arm.

name: Colt Emerson
tldr: Emerson has electric hitting hands and should be an everyday infielder. Despite our initial hesitance, his chances of hitting for power and staying at short have improved year over year.

name: JJ Wetherholt
tldr: Wetherholt is a bit too bulky and tightly-wound to stick at short, but he has the hit-and-power combination to be a star at the keystone.

name: Franklin Arias
tldr: Arias projects as a plus defender at short with a plus hit tool, making him one of the highest-floored prospects in the game.

name: Eli Willits
tldr: Willits was the top pick in the 2025 draft because of his defense, contact feel, and youth. He projects as a good leadoff hitter and plus defender.

name: Chase DeLauter
tldr: A litany of injuries have made it tough for DeLauter to stay on the field. When he's healthy, he looks like a heart-of-the-order bat capable of hitting for average and power.

name: Payton Tolle
tldr: Tolle's a huge, boxy guy with a monster fastball. The development of his secondaries will determine where he best fits in the rotation long-term.

name: Andrew Painter
tldr: Painter struggled to locate his secondary stuff for chase in 2025. He had a good season for a player his age at Triple-A, but didn't dominate and kick down the door to the bigs as hoped. Can he bounce back in 2026, or is he going to be just okay?

name: Ryan Sloan
tldr: Sloan was one of the top arms in the low minors last year. He could have three plus pitches at maturity.

name: Bryce Eldridge
tldr: Eldridge is an enormous power-hitting first base prospect whose swing is already geared to get to his tremendous raw thunder.

name: Josue De Paula
tldr: De Paula has an ideal slugging corner outfield frame paired with a good batting eye, but he has yet to fully actualize his massive power potential.

name: Alfredo Duno
tldr: Duno is a tooled-up catcher with big power and incredible athleticism for a guy his size. He's likely to be a slower developmental burn than other elite prospects, but he has massive long-term ceiling.

name: Luis Peña
tldr: Peña is a twitchy, projectable infielder with a chance for an average bat and plus game power. He probably isn't a shortstop, though.

name: Rainiel Rodriguez
tldr: Built like a short-levered Michelin man, Rodriguez bashed his way into affiliated ball with precocious pull power, and has the tools and smarts to stick behind the plate.

name: Carson Benge
tldr: Benge has one of the sweetest swings in the minors and a shot to be a five-tool player, though he lacks any one elite tool.

name: Liam Doyle
tldr: Doyle is a superb athlete who broke out in his draft year at Tennessee thanks to a 70-grade fastball, with enough fledgling feel for his secondaries to stick as a starter.

name: Carson Williams
tldr: Williams has plus power and a potential 70-grade glove at short, but there's enough swing-and-miss that there's more bust potential than his FV grade would normally suggest.

name: Bryce Rainer
tldr: Rainer was a two-way high school player whose stock took off as a shortstop fairly late during the pre-draft process. He was hitting with power in 2025 before he hurt his shoulder and required surgery.

name: Aidan Miller
tldr: Miller is a well-rounded shortstop prospect with uncommon power.

name: Jarlin Susana
tldr: Susana has one of the planet's nastiest sliders and touches 103. He'll miss the first half of 2026 recovering from lat surgery.

name: Sebastian Walcott
tldr: Walcott looked like he was joining the Tatis Cinematic Universe in 2023 and 2024, but had only a fair 2025 and then got hurt.

name: Walker Jenkins
tldr: Jenkins is an oft-injured outfielder with an excellent approach and contact feel, and he's improved in center field. He doesn't have the power of a superstar player and looks more like if Jesse Winker could play better defense.

name: Carter Jensen
tldr: Jensen is a power-hitting lefty catcher who squares up the ball as reliably as any prospect in the game. He projects to grow into an average defender and is big-league ready.

name: Caleb Bonemer
tldr: Bonemer is unlikely to stick at short, but the Michigan prep star already looked like a polished all-around bat in his first full season of pro ball.

name: Sal Stewart
tldr: Other than pulling fastballs, Stewart does everything well at the plate, which should allow him to be a regular at first base, his likely defensive home.

name: Ryan Waldschmidt
tldr: Waldschmidt is a well-rounded corner bat with 30-homer upside.

name: Noah Schultz
tldr: Schultz was troubled by patellar tendinitis in 2025 and has a checkered health history, but he has largely thrown strikes and touts a plus slider from a freaky XL left-handed frame.

name: Brandon Sproat
tldr: Sproat's delivery changed in 2025 and his changeup quality evaporated. If he can get back to the way he looked from 2020-2024, he'll be a big part of the Brewers rotation soon.

name: Connelly Early
tldr: An elite competitor with a deceptively good fastball, Early is a big league-ready mid-rotation starter.

name: Dylan Beavers
tldr: Beavers is a corner outfielder with power and a good approach. Long levers and a tendency to work deep counts may limit his ability to hit for average.

name: Moisés Ballesteros
tldr: Ballesteros can really hit. Defensively, well, he can really hit.

name: Ralphy Velazquez
tldr: Velazquez broke out in 2025 and now projects as a regular first baseman with 30-homer upside and a good approach.

name: Angel Genao
tldr: Genao is a well-rounded shortstop who is still growing into meaningful power.

name: Eduardo Quintero
tldr: Quintero is a fine center field defender with a lovely gap-to-gap approach. If he can start turning on the baseball more frequently, he might end up with enough power to be a 55- or 60-grade prospect a year from now.

name: Josuar Gonzalez
tldr: The top prospect in the DSL last year, Gonzalez projects as an above-average hitter and defender at short.

name: Seth Hernandez
tldr: Hernandez is one of the better high school pitchers of the last decade, a freak athlete who'll touch 100 with ease before finishing hitters with a great changeup. His breaking ball and control need polish.

name: Eduardo Tait
tldr: Tait is a power-hitting catcher with a laser arm and a concerning lack of plate discipline.

name: Ethan Holliday
tldr: Holliday has absurd raw power and a long swing that has limited his ability to make consistent contact. He might need an adjustment to break out, and he has 40-homer ceiling if he does.

name: Arjun Nimmala
tldr: Nimmala is a physical shortstop with power, but also hit tool risk.

name: George Lombard Jr.
tldr: Lombard is an acrobatic shortstop defender with a chance to hit for power.

name: Parker Messick
tldr: Messick has profiled as a low-variance no. 4 lefty starter with a plus changeup for a while now, and he looked the part during his seven-start debut in 2025.

name: Kade Anderson
tldr: Maybe the best pitcher in the 2025 draft, Anderson projects as a quick-moving mid-rotation starter with a great breaking ball.

name: Jonny Farmelo
tldr: Farmelo's raw power and speed give him immense potential if he can continue to refine his swing in pro ball. Injuries have limited his ability to get going and threaten his chances to stay in center.

name: Tyler Bremner
tldr: A 70 changeup highlights a relatively safe, no. 3 projection for Bremner. Blink and he'll be in the Angels rotation.

name: Elmer Rodríguez
tldr: Rodríguez projects as a mid-rotation starter, largely on his ability to generate groundballs with his sinker.

name: Travis Bazzana
tldr: The first overall pick in the 2024 draft, Bazzana is a small-ish second baseman who is a good bet to be an average everyday player, but probably not a star.

name: Jefferson Rojas
tldr: Rojas is a young middle infield prospect with a great looking swing and increasingly slick shortstop defense.

name: Joe Mack
tldr: Mack is a power-hitting lefty catcher with a plus-plus arm. A long swing and corresponding hit risk likely pushes him to the back of the batting order.

name: Emil Morales
tldr: Young, twitchy, and explosive, Morales is one of the highest-variance prospects in baseball. You can dream on a 25-homer shortstop.

name: Carlos Lagrange
tldr: Lagrange has a big arm and could have two plus-plus pitches in relief. We're projecting him as a closer.

name: Jonah Tong
tldr: Tong is a great athlete with a plus fastball-changeup mix. A complicated delivery and iffy breaking ball projection cloud his long-term projection.

name: Brody Hopkins
tldr: Hopkins only began pitching in a serious way during his draft spring. The Mariners traded him for Randy Arozarena, and Hopkins has become a hard-throwing power pitcher with a nasty cutter under the Rays' tutelage.

name: Luis De León
tldr: De León has easy mid-90s lefty velo and a suite of plus secondaries, but he's good for one stretch of losing the strike zone per season.

name: Hagen Smith
tldr: Smith's compact arm stroke offers one of the best left-handed fastball/slider combos in the minors, but he has only thrown a starter-level of strikes in his draft year at Arkansas.

name: Owen Caissie
tldr: Elite power makes Caissie's K-prone skill set viable in an outfield corner.

name: Charlie Condon
tldr: With two hand injuries in the rear-view mirror, it's time for Condon to prove he can tap into the plus-plus power that made him such a coveted amateur prospect.

name: Zyhir Hope
tldr: Hope is a risky corner outfielder with serious hit tool questions, but also 40-homer upside if everything clicks.

name: Lazaro Montes
tldr: Montes has a classic power-hitting profile. He's among the bigger boom-bust types in the sport.

name: JR Ritchie
tldr: The arm strength scouts have been projecting Ritchie to develop arrived last year. He's a near-ready no. 3/4 starter.

name: Dax Kilby
tldr: Kilby's 2025 pro debut reinforced pre-existing confidence that he could hit, and he also looked better playing shortstop than he did in high school. He now looks like a shrewd selection.

name: Yolfran Castillo
tldr: Castillo is an exciting young hitter with a rare combination of contact ability and physical projection.

name: Caden Scarborough
tldr: Scarborough is a lanky, projectable righty with a plus fastball/sweeper combo. He took a leap as a strike-thrower in 2025.

name: Didier Fuentes
tldr: Fuentes has been promoted quickly, and perhaps wasn't ready for his big league debut. A good fastball, well-rounded mix, and projectable command give him mid-rotation upside.

name: Robby Snelling
tldr: Snelling went to a third party facility and changed his delivery, then had a proper breakout in 2025. He's now on track to be in Miami's rotation during the next year or so.

name: Gage Jump
tldr: Jump made strike-throwing improvements and demonstrated 100-inning stamina in 2025, putting him on track to be a fastball-heavy mid-rotation starter.

name: George Klassen
tldr: Klassen has always had big stuff, and now he's hitting the zone often enough to project him as a good five-and-dive type.

name: Harry Ford
tldr: Ford is a laser-armed catcher who, brick-by-brick, has become a major league-quality receiver. Selectivity at the dish helps him do enough to be a primary catcher.

name: Jett Williams
tldr: Williams is a compact little speedster with surprising power for his size. He is still searching the 2B/SS/CF triangle for a comfortable defensive home.

name: Jeferson Quero
tldr: Quero has plus bat speed and arm strength, but a volatile approach.

name: Jhonny Level
tldr: Level was arguably the best prospect in the ACL last year. He isn't a big guy, but he can hit, has sneaky power, and projects to stay at short.

name: Michael Arroyo
tldr: Arroyo is a short-levered, power-hitting second baseman with a good approach and a shaky glove.

name: Jack Wenninger
tldr: Wenninger found more velocity last year; he now projects as a no. 4 starter.

name: Aiva Arquette
tldr: Arquette is an ultra passive hitter with rare size and thump for a shortstop, though he didn't hit for power in his post-draft sample.

name: Logan Henderson
tldr: Henderson is a ready-made no. 4 starter with a modern pitcher's arsenal, headlined by a carrying fastball.

name: David Davalillo
tldr: Davalillo is a crafty starter prospect with great secondary stuff, led by his plus-plus splitter.

name: Khal Stephen
tldr: Stephen throws a ton of strikes and has plus command of mostly average stuff.

name: Michael Forret
tldr: Forret is a command-oriented starter whose pitch execution allows his modest stuff to play up. He's a pitchability no. 4 starter prospect.

name: Jimmy Crooks
tldr: Crooks is a polished defensive catcher with a below-average bat, but he offers the brute strength to produce double-digit home run seasons.

name: Jurrangelo Cijntje
tldr: Cijntje has a shot to be a switch-pitcher, but his righty stuff might be good enough on its own to make him a mid-rotation starter.

name: Christian Oppor
tldr: Oppor is an athletic JUCO lefty who has two bat-missing secondaries and has touched 100 mph, but he has a limited track record of strikes and performance.

name: Kyson Witherspoon
tldr: Upper-90s arm strength and plus breaking stuff spearheads Witherspoon's mid-rotation projection.

name: Kaelen Culpepper
tldr: Culpepper is an acrobatic, if unrefined, defender at short who is already realizing his 20-homer power despite persistent chase issues.

name: Tommy Troy
tldr: A bounce-back year on both sides of the ball has Troy tracking like a regular at second base with a multi-positional utility fallback if the bat falls short.

name: Roldy Brito
tldr: A young spark plug with speed and sneaky power, Brito is tooled up and has a chance to be very good at multiple spots around the diamond. To call him a super-utility prospect almost doesn't do him justice.

name: Felnin Celesten
tldr: Celesten is a physical, switch-hitting middle infield prospect. Brimming with potential, injuries have limited his chances to develop baseball skills alongside his enviable tools.

name: Braden Montgomery
tldr: Montgomery is a yoked switch-hitting outfielder who mashed in college and touts huge bat speed, but he has enough swing-and-miss that there's bust potential.

name: Jacob Reimer
tldr: Reimer is a well-rounded hitter who projects as an everyday third baseman.

name: Edward Florentino
tldr: Florentino is a young power-hitting prospect. Defensively, he's likely to wind up in a corner.

name: Rhett Lowder
tldr: Lowder is a fourth starter prospect who attacks east/west with a sinker and slider.

name: Winston Santos
tldr: Santos is an athletic, strike-throwing starter with a mid-90s fastball, but pedestrian secondary stuff.

name: Jaxon Wiggins
tldr: Wiggins has one of the more dominant fastballs in the minors, and his changeup has improved in pro ball. Can he stay healthy and throw enough strikes to start?

name: Tanner McDougal
tldr: McDougal is a big-bodied right-hander with plus velocity and two bat-missing breaking balls. His lack of changeup feel and delivery effort pose substantial relief risk.

name: Blake Mitchell
tldr: Mitchell is an athletic catcher who has grown defensively since draft day. A broken hamate last year has clouded his projection at the plate, where he looks like a power-over-hit player.

name: Ethan Salas
tldr: Salas had an early-career breakout, but has either struggled or been injured for the last couple of seasons.

name: Thayron Liranzo
tldr: Liranzo has huge power and big questions to answer on both sides of the ball after a rough ride at Double-A in 2025.

name: Josue Briceño
tldr: Briceño is a well-rounded hitter who reached Double-A in 2025. He's still catching some, but he's probably going to be a first baseman or DH.

name: River Ryan
tldr: Ryan looked like an impact starter before injuries ended his 2024 and wiped out his 2025.

name: Jake Bloss
tldr: Bloss has a riding mid-90s fastball and a great pair of breaking balls that he was just starting to harness before he blew out. He had surgery in May of 2025 and should pitch in the second half of 2026.

name: Travis Sykora
tldr: Sykora is an enormous, hard-throwing righty with an excellent splitter who is currently on the shelf with injury. He looked like a potential no. 2/3 starter when healthy.
